---
title: "Research Tracks — Where This Course Continues"
description: "What sits beyond the course: two long-form research projects this curriculum is designed to enable."
---

The course you've been working through is paced — ten weeks of mathematics, eight controversies, exercises and mini-projects designed to get you reading the Marxian-computational tradition with full mathematical comprehension. That work is bounded: a syllabus, a finishable arc, a set of skills you can verify you have. The research tracks are different.

A **research track** is long-form, slow, and publication-oriented. There is no syllabus, no fixed pacing, no exercises. The format is reading and reflection: working notes that accumulate, position papers as they accrete, occasional simulations where they help clarify a claim, and an evolving annotated bibliography. The output is artifacts — papers, libraries, citable preprints — not graded work. The point is to take what the course taught you and bring it into contact with open questions where the answers are genuinely contested.

Two research tracks exist downstream of this course. They sit in `research/` in the repo and deploy under `/research/<name>/` on the site. Both are presently stubs; the work is what fills them in.

## Marxian Formalization

[**Computational Reconstruction of Zhang's Marxian Formalization**](/research/marxian_formalization/)

Zhang Xian's 2023 *WRPE* paper sketches a mathematical formalization of *Capital* Vol I that is explicitly not Morishima's: it brings in game theory, probability, cybernetic control, and continuous-time differential equations alongside (and partly against) the non-negative-matrix apparatus of the Japanese School. Zhang writes the equations down but does not implement them; to the best of what can be established, nobody has. The track aims at a computationally rigorous companion to Zhang 2023 — a Jupyter notebook or small library that runs each of the models he sketches — with three plausible endpoints: a methodology paper for *WRPE* (co-authored with Zhang if he's interested), an open-source library that implements Morishima-Okishio, TSSI, and Zhang as alternative backends against the same economy object, or a Zhang-compatible extension of the Cockshott / Dapprich planning tradition.

**When to start.** After the [seminar between Controversies 5 and 6](controversies/seminar-chinese-critique.qmd). By then you'll have read Zhang's three English-language *WRPE* papers plus Bin Yu's companion piece, you'll know whether the critique lands for you, and you'll have real views about what the project should look like. Until then, the [research-track index](/research/marxian_formalization/) is a placeholder.

**Prerequisites.** All ten weeks; all seven controversies; the Chinese-critique seminar; Zhang 2023 read carefully. Light additional background in game theory, stochastic processes, and control theory is described in the [research-track index](/research/marxian_formalization/).

## Postcapitalism After AI

[**Postcapitalist Coordination and Planning After AI**](/research/postcapitalism_after_ai/)

A reading and writing program organized around a single animating question: *what does post-capitalist coordination look like when generative AI is the dominant general-purpose technology?* The track grows out of a five-piece exchange across *The Ideas Letter* and *Conflict & Democracy* in late 2025 and early 2026 — Morozov, Benanav, Weatherby, and Mason — and reaches outward into the conceptual hinterland that exchange does not itself supply: the post-1991 turn in neoliberal political economy, the technical-ideological history of optimization-as-computing, the empirical labor economics of agentic AI rollout, the Marxist value-form / Habermas-critical / Castoriadian / Echeverrian / Joasian pragmatist apparatus that lurks under the philosophical disagreements, and the parallel question of whether blockchain coordination infrastructure is a real alternative or another expression of the same complex.

The track is organized as **six threads**: the Calculation Debate after 1991; multi-criterial planning; optimization-economics-computing as one phenomenon; the empirical political economy of AI labor markets; the philosophical apparatus; and crypto / blockchain as coordination infrastructure (which absorbed the prior crypto-course stub). The threads are independent lines of work that cross-reference each other; they are not chapters in a sequence.

**When to start.** Earlier than the Marxian Formalization track. **Thread 1 is the direct sequel to [Controversy 2](controversies/controversy-02-calculation.qmd)** and can be opened as soon as that controversy is finished — Morozov's claim that the Calculation Debate was sublated in 1991 by Buchanan and Vanberg is precisely the contemporary follow-up that Controversy 2 begs for. The remaining five threads are best approached after Controversies 6–8, when the value-theoretic and macro foundations are in. Reading the five primary [source texts](/research/postcapitalism_after_ai/sources.qmd) end-to-end is the obvious first move.

**Prerequisites.** Controversy 2 minimum (for Thread 1); Controversies 6–8 for the rest; familiarity with the Postone / value-form / Habermas / Castoriadis lineage is assumed where it lands but not required to start.

## How they relate

The two tracks are not in competition. They sit on different parts of the conceptual map: Marxian Formalization is a *technical* extension — what does the Marxian apparatus look like under a richer mathematical toolkit, and can it be made computationally legible? Postcapitalism After AI is a *political-economic* extension — what does post-capitalist coordination look like under contemporary technological conditions that the classical debates didn't anticipate? Both presuppose the calculation-debate apparatus from Controversy 2 and the value-theoretic apparatus from the controversies that follow. Both are downstream; neither is a prerequisite for the other.

If you have to pick one to start, the question to ask is which kind of artifact you want to produce. Marxian Formalization wants code and a methodology paper. Postcapitalism After AI wants long-form synthesis and possibly small simulators that sharpen specific claims. Most likely you want to do both, in some interleaved order, and that's fine — the research register is wide enough to hold both.
